content:
# Genesis Chapter 24 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 13r–14r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_024_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 19–21 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now Abraham was old, well advanced in years, and the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things.

**2** And Abraham said to his servant, the oldest of his household, who had charge of all that he had, "Put your hand under my thigh,

**3** that I may make you swear by the LORD, the God of heaven and God of the earth, that you will not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell,

**4** but will go to my country and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son Isaac."

**5** The servant said to him, "Perhaps the woman will not be willing to follow me to this land. Must I then bring your son back to the land from which you came?"

**6** Abraham said to him, "See to it that you do not take my son back there.

**7** The LORD, the God of heaven, who took me from my father's house and from the land of my kindred, and who spoke to me and swore to me, 'To your offspring I will give this land' — he will send his angel before you, and you shall take a wife for my son from there.

**8** But if the woman is not willing to follow you, then you will be free from this oath of mine; only you must not take my son back there."

**9** So the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master and swore to him concerning this matter.

**10** Then the servant took ten of his master's camels and departed, taking all sorts of choice gifts from his master; and he arose and went to Mesopotamia, to the city of Nahor.

**11** And he made the camels kneel down outside the city by the well of water at the time of evening, the time when women go out to draw water.

**12** And he said, "O LORD, God of my master Abraham, please grant me success today and show steadfast love to my master Abraham.

**13** Behold, I am standing by the spring of water, and the daughters of the men of the city are coming out to draw water.

**14** Let the young woman to whom I shall say, 'Please let down your jar that I may drink,' and who shall say, 'Drink, and I will water your camels also' — let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant Isaac. By this I shall know that you have shown steadfast love to my master."

**15** Before he had finished speaking, behold, Rebekah, who was born to Bethuel the son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother, came out with her water jar on her shoulder.

**16** The young woman was very attractive in appearance, a virgin, whom no man had known. She went down to the spring and filled her jar and came up.

**17** Then the servant ran to meet her and said, "Please give me a little water to drink from your jar."

**18** She said, "Drink, my lord." And she quickly let down her jar upon her hand and gave him a drink.

**19** When she had finished giving him a drink, she said, "I will draw water for your camels also, until they have finished drinking."

**20** So she quickly emptied her jar into the trough and ran again to the well to draw water, and she drew for all his camels.

**21** The man gazed at her in silence, waiting to know whether the LORD had prospered his journey or not.

**22** When the camels had finished drinking, the man took a gold ring weighing a half shekel, and two bracelets for her arms weighing ten gold shekels,

**23** and said, "Please tell me whose daughter you are. Is there room in your father's house for us to lodge?"

**24** She said to him, "I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, whom she bore to Nahor."

**25** She added, "We have plenty of both straw and fodder, and room to lodge."

**26** The man bowed his head and worshiped the LORD

**27** and said, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not forsaken his steadfast love and his faithfulness toward my master. As for me, the LORD has led me in the way to the house of my master's kinsmen."

**28** Then the young woman ran and told her mother's household about these things.

**29** Rebekah had a brother whose name was Laban. Laban ran out toward the man, to the spring.

**30** As soon as he saw the ring and the bracelets on his sister's arms, and heard the words of Rebekah his sister, "Thus the man spoke to me," he went to the man. And behold, he was standing by the camels at the spring.

**31** He said, "Come in, O blessed of the LORD. Why do you stand outside? For I have prepared the house and a place for the camels."

**32** So the man came to the house and unharnessed the camels, and gave straw and fodder to the camels, and there was water to wash his feet and the feet of the men who were with him.

**33** Then food was set before him to eat. But he said, "I will not eat until I have said what I have to say." He said, "Speak on."

**34** So he said, "I am Abraham's servant.

**35** The LORD has greatly blessed my master, and he has become great. He has given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male servants and female servants, camels and donkeys.

**36** And Sarah my master's wife bore a son to my master when she was old, and to him he has given all that he has.

**37** My master made me swear, saying, 'You shall not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell,

**38** but you shall go to my father's house and to my clan and take a wife for my son.'

**39** I said to my master, 'Perhaps the woman will not follow me.'

**40** But he said to me, 'The LORD, before whom I have walked, will send his angel with you and prosper your way. You shall take a wife for my son from my clan and from my father's house.

**41** Then you will be free from my oath, when you come to my clan. And if they will not give her to you, you will be free from my oath.'

**42** "I came today to the spring and said, 'O LORD, the God of my master Abraham, if now you are prospering the way that I go,

**43** behold, I am standing by the spring of water. Let the virgin who comes out to draw water, to whom I shall say, "Please give me a little water from your jar to drink,"

**44** and who will say to me, "Drink, and I will draw for your camels also" — let her be the woman whom the LORD has appointed for my master's son.'

**45** "Before I had finished speaking in my heart, behold, Rebekah came out with her water jar on her shoulder, and she went down to the spring and drew water. I said to her, 'Please let me drink.'

**46** She quickly let down her jar from her shoulder and said, 'Drink, and I will water your camels also.' So I drank, and she watered the camels also.

**47** Then I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?' She said, 'The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor's son, whom Milcah bore to him.' So I put the ring on her nose and the bracelets on her arms.

**48** Then I bowed my head and worshiped the LORD and blessed the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me by the right way to take the daughter of my master's kinsman for his son.

**49** Now then, if you are going to show steadfast love and faithfulness to my master, tell me; and if not, tell me, that I may turn to the right hand or to the left."

**50** Then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, "The thing has come from the LORD; we cannot speak to you bad or good.

**51** Behold, Rebekah is before you; take her and go, and let her be the wife of your master's son, as the LORD has spoken."

**52** When Abraham's servant heard their words, he bowed himself to the earth before the LORD.

**53** And the servant brought out jewelry of silver and of gold, and garments, and gave them to Rebekah. He also gave to her brother and to her mother costly ornaments.

**54** And he and the men who were with him ate and drank, and they spent the night there. When they arose in the morning, he said, "Send me away to my master."

**55** Her brother and her mother said, "Let the young woman remain with us a while, at least ten days; after that she may go."

**56** But he said to them, "Do not delay me, since the LORD has prospered my way. Send me away that I may go to my master."

**57** They said, "Let us call the young woman and ask her."

**58** And they called Rebekah and said to her, "Will you go with this man?" She said, "I will go."

**59** So they sent away Rebekah their sister and her nurse, and Abraham's servant and his men.

**60** And they blessed Rebekah and said to her, "Our sister, may you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your offspring possess the gate of those who hate him!"

**61** Then Rebekah and her young women arose and rode on the camels and followed the man. Thus the servant took Rebekah and went his way.

**62** Now Isaac had come from Beer-lahai-roi and was dwelling in the Negeb.

**63** And Isaac went out to meditate in the field toward evening. And he lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, there were camels coming.

**64** And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she dismounted from the camel

**65** and said to the servant, "Who is that man, walking in the field to meet us?" The servant said, "It is my master." So she took her veil and covered herself.

**66** And the servant told Isaac all the things that he had done.

**67** Then Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother and took Rebekah, and she became his wife, and he loved her. So Isaac was comforted after his mother's death.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of five manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 24 spans Folios 13r–14r across three pages.

   **Folio 13r** (GEN_024_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 13. The closing verses of Genesis 23 appear at the top of the right-hand column. The chapter section for Genesis 24 begins on this page (the petuchah/open paragraph marker visible in the Masoretic layout). The three-column Tiberian format is clearly legible, with full Masoretic pointing (niqqud) and cantillation marks (te'amim). The page carries Genesis 24 approximately through verse 22, covering Abraham's commission of the servant, the oath-ceremony, the servant's departure, his prayer at the well, and Rebekah's appearance. The folio number "13" is visible in the upper margin.

   **Folio 13v** (GEN_024_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 13. This page is presented in inverted orientation in the photograph (the WSRP imaging convention). The text runs in three columns and carries Genesis 24 from approximately verse 23 through approximately verse 49 — comprising the exchange of gifts and questions, Rebekah's identification, Laban's welcome, the meal at Rebekah's house, and the servant's full retelling of his commission and journey. The Masoretic text with pointing is fully legible throughout.

   **Folio 14r** (GEN_024_page_003.jpg): The recto of Folio 14. The folio number "14" appears in the upper margin. This page carries Genesis 24:50 through Genesis 24:67, then continues into the opening of Genesis 25. It covers the family's agreement, the gift-giving, the departure negotiation, Rebekah's consent, the blessing formula, the journey south, Isaac's meeting with Rebekah, and the closing verse. The transition to Genesis 25 is visible near the bottom of the page.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Three pages were read directly.

   **Page 19** (GEN_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): The chapter marker "CAP. XXVII" appears in the left margin toward the bottom of the page (reflecting the LXX chapter division system). The upper portion of page 19 carries the closing material of Genesis 23. Genesis 24 in the LXX begins on this page and runs through approximately verse 27 in the continuous three-column uncial hand characteristic of Vaticanus.

   **Page 20** (GEN_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): The chapter marker "CAP. XXVIII" is visible in the left margin, marking the LXX division. The continuous uncial text carries approximately Genesis 24:28 through 24:60, encompassing the household reception, the servant's retelling, and the negotiation of Rebekah's departure.

   **Page 21** (GEN_024_vaticanus_page_003.jpg): The chapter marker "CAP. XXIX" appears at the top of the page. This page carries Genesis 24:61 through the close of chapter 24 and the beginning of Genesis 25. The concluding verses of the chapter — Isaac's meeting with Rebekah and the final verse — are legible in the third column of this page.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. זָקֵן בָּא בַּיָּמִים — "Well Advanced in Years" (v. 1)**

The Hebrew phrase בָּא בַּיָּמִים (ba' bayyamim, literally "come in [with] the days") is an idiom for advanced age, appearing also of Abraham in 18:11 and of Joshua in Joshua 13:1 and 23:1. The verb בָּא (ba', "to come, to enter") with בַּיָּמִים ("in/with the days") suggests that a person has fully entered into the span of their days — that old age has arrived and taken up residence. The formal translation renders "well advanced in years" (following ESV, NASB), which captures the idiom naturally without over-literalizing "come into days." The phrase combined with זָקֵן (zaqen, "old, elderly") creates a double emphasis: both the simple fact of old age and the quality of deep elderliness. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: πρεσβύτερος προβεβηκὼς ἡμερῶν (presbuteros probebēkōs hēmerōn — "an elder who has advanced in days"), preserving the "advanced in days" idiom quite literally.

**2. שִׂים נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי — "Put Your Hand Under My Thigh" (v. 2)**

This is one of the most discussed oath-gestures in the Hebrew Bible. The formula שִׂים יָד תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ (sim yad takhat yerekh, "put [your] hand under the thigh") occurs only here and in Genesis 47:29, where Jacob asks Joseph to swear the same oath before his death. The gesture involves placing one's hand beneath the thigh — almost certainly a euphemism for the genitalia or the procreative region — of the one to whom the oath is sworn.

Ancient Near Eastern parallels suggest that swearing by touching the procreative organ of a superior or ancestor invoked the power of that person's lineage and future offspring as the sanction for the oath. To swear by Abraham's thigh is to swear by Abraham's seed — by the very covenant promise that runs through his body, the promise of descendants as numerous as the stars. The oath is thus not merely a social formality; it is a covenantal act that implicates the entire future of the Abrahamic line.

The formal translation renders "Put your hand under my thigh" — the literal Hebrew formula — following ESV, NASB, KJV, and the entire formal tradition. This preserves the archaic gravity of the gesture. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ὑπὸ τὸν μηρόν μου (hypo ton mēron mou — "under my thigh"), translating directly without euphemism.

**3. אֵל הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ — "God of Heaven and God of the Earth" (v. 3)**

The full divine title in Abraham's oath-formula: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ (YHWH Elohei hashamayim velohei ha'aretz — "the LORD, God of the heavens and God of the earth") presents YHWH as sovereign over the total created order — both the celestial and the terrestrial realms. The double "God of" construction is emphatic. The formal translation renders "the LORD, the God of heaven and God of the earth" — preserving the double construction. Note that in verse 7, Abraham drops "and God of the earth," using only "the LORD, the God of heaven" in the abbreviated form he uses when speaking to the servant of YHWH's own direct action in his life. This stylistic variation between the oath-formula (v. 3) and the personal recollection (v. 7) is preserved in the translation. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads: κύριον τὸν θεὸν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ τὸν θεὸν τῆς γῆς (kyrion ton theon tou ouranou kai ton theon tēs gēs — "the Lord, the God of heaven and the God of the earth"), fully parallel to the MT.

**4. אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם — "Mesopotamia" (v. 10)**

The Hebrew toponym אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם (Aram naharayim — literally "Aram of the two rivers") designates the upper Mesopotamian region between the Euphrates and its tributaries — roughly corresponding to northwestern modern Syria/southeastern Turkey, including the area known in Assyrian sources as Nahrima. The patriarchal city of Nahor (עִיר נָחוֹר, ir Nahor) is likely to be identified with Haran (cf. Genesis 11:31–12:4; 27:43; 28:10) or a nearby settlement in the same region. The LXX renders Μεσοποταμίαν Συρίας (Mesopotamian Syrias — "Mesopotamia of Syria"), adding "of Syria" as a geographical specification. The formal translation renders "Mesopotamia" as the standard English rendering of Aram naharayim, following the entire formal tradition, while noting the more literal meaning in these notes.

**5. חֶסֶד — "Steadfast Love" (vv. 12, 14, 27, 49)**

The Hebrew noun חֶסֶד (hesed) is one of the most theologically significant and lexically complex words in the Hebrew Bible. Its semantic range encompasses: loyalty, faithfulness, covenant-love, kindness, mercy, and steadfast commitment within a relationship. In English translation it has been rendered variously as "mercy" (KJV), "lovingkindness" (KJV marginal, NASB), "steadfast love" (ESV, RSV), "unfailing love" (NIV), and "faithful love" (NLT).

In Genesis 24, hesed appears in the servant's prayer (v. 12 — "show steadfast love to my master Abraham"), in the sign-test formula (v. 14 — "you have shown steadfast love to my master"), in the servant's doxology at the well (v. 27 — "who has not forsaken his steadfast love and his faithfulness toward my master"), and in the servant's challenge to Laban and Bethuel (v. 49 — "if you are going to show steadfast love and faithfulness to my master"). The pairing חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת (hesed ve'emet — "steadfast love and faithfulness") in verses 27 and 49 is a fixed hendiadys in biblical Hebrew for covenant loyalty in its full character: the affective commitment (hesed) and its reliable, trustworthy quality (emet). This same pairing appears in Psalm 25:10, 85:10, 89:14, and is echoed in John 1:14 (χάριτος καὶ ἀληθείας, "grace and truth").

The formal translation renders hesed consistently as "steadfast love" throughout the chapter, following ESV convention, and renders the hendiadys as "steadfast love and faithfulness" in verses 27 and 49.

**6. וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ — "The Man Gazed at Her in Silence" (v. 21)**

The verb מִשְׁתָּאֵה is the hitpael participle of שָׁאָה — a rare form appearing only here and in Isaiah 41:23 and Psalm 40:15 (in related forms). The root conveys a state of rapt attention, silent astonishment, or intent watching. The hitpael (reflexive-intensive) form מִשְׁתָּאֵה intensifies this further: the man is not merely looking at Rebekah; he is in a state of concentrated, almost suspended observation — simultaneously watching her work and internally waiting for divine confirmation.

The adverb מַחֲרִישׁ (maharish, "in silence, keeping silent") accompanies the participle, confirming that this is not merely intent visual focus but a moment of held breath — the servant is silent, watching, suspended between hope and uncertainty. The formal translation renders "gazed at her in silence" — preserving both the visual intensity (gazed) and the interior silence (in silence). The LXX renders κατεμάνθανεν αὐτήν (katemanthanen autēn — "he was observing/studying her carefully"), using the compound of μανθάνω that conveys attentive learning-observation. This is an apt rendering that captures the careful, intentional quality of the servant's scrutiny.

**7. The Servant's Retelling (vv. 34–49) — Narrative Repetition with Variation**

The servant's speech to Laban and Bethuel in verses 34–49 is a masterpiece of Hebrew narrative art. He retells the entire story of his commission and journey — events already narrated in verses 1–27 — but with significant alterations:

- In verse 47, the servant rearranges the order of events: in the narrative of verses 22–23, he first gave Rebekah the ring and bracelets and then asked her name; in the retelling (v. 47), he reverses the order, saying "I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?' ... So I put the ring on her nose and the bracelets on her arms." The servant presents himself as having asked her identity before giving the gifts — a more prudent sequence that reflects better on his judgment when speaking to her family.

- The phrase "in my heart" in verse 45 (אֲדַבֵּר אֶל לִבִּי, "speaking to my heart") indicates that the servant's prayer at the well was an interior prayer — said in his heart rather than aloud. This detail is absent from the narrative version (v. 12–14), where the prayer is presented directly without specifying whether it was audible or silent. The retelling adds this interiority.

- The LXX (Vaticanus) follows the MT's retelling closely, preserving these narrative variations without harmonizing them to the earlier account.

The formal translation preserves both the original narrative and the retelling with their differences intact, following the MT without harmonization.

**8. הַנֶּזֶם — "Ring" and the Nose-Ring Detail (vv. 22, 47)**

The word נֶזֶם (nezem) in verse 22 is most naturally translated "ring," but its specific meaning in context is important. In verse 47, the servant explicitly states וָאָשִׂם הַנֶּזֶם עַל אַפָּהּ (va'asim hanezem al appah — "and I put the ring on her nose"), confirming that this was a nose ring — a common form of jewelry in the ancient Near East for women (cf. Ezekiel 16:12, Isaiah 3:21, Proverbs 11:22). The manifest in verse 22 describes it as weighing a half-shekel of gold (בֶּקַע זָהָב — beqa' zahav, "a half-shekel weight of gold"), making it a substantial piece. The formal translation renders "ring" in verse 22 (where the placement is not yet specified in the narrative) and preserves "on her nose" in verse 47 (where the retelling specifies the placement), following the MT's own narratological logic.

**9. לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה — "To Meditate in the Field" (v. 63)**

The verb לָשׂוּחַ (lasuach) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible in this form. The root שִׂיחַ (siach) elsewhere means "to muse, meditate, speak, converse, complain" (cf. Psalm 55:17, 77:3, 119:15, 23, 27, 48, 78, etc., where the root governs meditative recitation and interior speech). The hapax לָשׂוּחַ has generated considerable discussion:

- The most common rendering is "to meditate" (ESV, KJV: "to meditate"; NASB: "to meditate / to pray"), reading it as Isaac's practice of evening meditation or prayer — a natural reading given the root's association with contemplative inner speech in the Psalms.
- Some scholars propose "to walk about / to stroll" (reading the form as cognate to an Aramaic root for walking about), connecting it to the evening setting and Isaac's going out into the field.
- A few proposals connect it to "to converse" — Isaac going out to speak (to God? alone?).

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἀδολεσχῆσαι (adoleschēsai — "to engage in prolonged meditation / to ruminate"), using a verb that in Greek describes absorbed, protracted thought or speech — very close to the meditative meaning. This is significant: the LXX translator working from a Hebrew text in the 3rd-2nd century BCE understood the hapax as "to meditate/contemplate," lending weight to that reading.

The formal translation renders "to meditate" — following ESV, the LXX witness, and the root's consistent association with contemplative inner speech throughout the Psalter. This is the most defensible rendering. The evening setting (לִפְנוֹת עֶרֶב, "toward evening") evokes the traditional time of prayer and reflection.

**10. וְיִנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ — "Comforted After His Mother's Death" (v. 67)**

The final clause of Genesis 24 is emotionally the most resonant in the chapter. The verb נָחַם (nakham, Niphal: vayinnakhem — "and he was comforted") is the word used throughout the Hebrew Bible for consolation from grief (cf. Genesis 37:35; 38:12; 2 Samuel 13:39). The phrase אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ (akharei immo, literally "after his mother") — "after" in the sense of "following [the death of]" — completes the emotional arc not just of this chapter but of the preceding one: Genesis 23 records Sarah's death; Genesis 24 closes with Isaac's grief for Sarah being consoled through his love for Rebekah.

The formal translation renders "So Isaac was comforted after his mother's death" — slightly expanding "after his mother" with "death" to make explicit what the Hebrew leaves implicit (that she is dead, not merely absent). This follows ESV, NASB, and the broader formal tradition. The LXX renders: παρεκλήθη Ισαακ περὶ τῆς μητρὸς αὐτοῦ (pareklēthē Isaak peri tēs mētros autou — "Isaac was consoled concerning/about his mother"), using παρακαλέω (parakaleō — the NT's primary word for consolation and encouragement).

**11. בֵּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי — "Beer-lahai-roi" (v. 62)**

The well Beer-lahai-roi — "the well of the Living One who sees me" — was first introduced in Genesis 16:14, where Hagar named it after her encounter with the angel of the LORD. That Isaac is dwelling by this well at the close of Genesis 24 is narratively significant: the well of divine seeing and provision, associated with Hagar and Ishmael, is now Isaac's dwelling place in the south. The Negeb (הַנֶּגֶב, hanegev — "the south, the dry land") is the arid southern region of Canaan where Isaac will dwell (cf. Genesis 26:17–22). The narrative detail grounds the romantic resolution in a specific geography that the careful reader will recognize from earlier in the Abraham cycle.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 24

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 19–21 were read directly. The chapter transitions in the LXX division system (CAP. XXVII, XXVIII, XXIX visible in the left margins across these pages) reflect the LXX's different chapter arrangement from the MT's. The continuous uncial text of Vaticanus follows the MT narrative closely but carries several notable variants and renderings:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 24:1 | בָּא בַּיָּמִים ("come in the days" — advanced in years) | προβεβηκὼς ἡμερῶν ("having advanced in days") | "well advanced in years" | LXX renders the Hebrew idiom literally, preserving "days"; formal translation follows the English idiom |
| 24:3 | אֵל הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ ("God of heaven and God of the earth") | κύριον τὸν θεὸν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ τὸν θεὸν τῆς γῆς ("the Lord, the God of heaven and the God of the earth") | "the LORD, the God of heaven and God of the earth" | LXX preserves the double construction fully parallel to MT |
| 24:10 | אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם ("Aram of the two rivers") | Μεσοποταμίαν Συρίας ("Mesopotamia of Syria") | "Mesopotamia" | LXX adds "of Syria" as a geographical specification; formal translation follows the standard English rendering of the Hebrew toponym |
| 24:12 | עֲשֵׂה חֶסֶד עִם אֲדֹנִי ("show steadfast love with my master") | ποίησον ἔλεος μετὰ τοῦ κυρίου μου ("show mercy with my master") | "show steadfast love to my master Abraham" | LXX renders חֶסֶד (hesed) as ἔλεος (eleos, "mercy") — its standard rendering for hesed throughout the LXX; formal translation follows "steadfast love" as the more precise covenant-loyalty term |
| 24:21 | מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ ("gazing at her") | κατεμάνθανεν αὐτήν ("observing/studying her carefully") | "gazed at her in silence" | LXX captures the attentive, intentional quality of the servant's observation with κατα-μανθάνω; the MT's מַחֲרִישׁ ("in silence") is also rendered |
| 24:47 | וָאָשִׂם הַנֶּזֶם עַל אַפָּהּ ("and I put the ring on her nose") | καὶ ἐπέθηκα αὐτῇ τὸν σταθμὸν ἐπὶ τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτῆς ("and I placed the weight/ornament on her face") | "I put the ring on her nose" | LXX uses πρόσωπον ("face") instead of specifying "nose"; the formal translation follows the MT's specificity |
| 24:55 | יָמִים אוֹ עָשׂוֹר ("days or ten [days]") | ἡμέρας ὡσεὶ δέκα ("days about ten") | "at least ten days" | LXX reads the Hebrew idiom as "about ten days"; formal translation renders "at least ten days" following the MT's sense of a minimum period |
| 24:63 | לָשׂוּחַ ("to meditate" — hapax) | ἀδολεσχῆσαι ("to meditate/ruminate") | "to meditate" | LXX's rendering strongly supports the meditative meaning; this is the only ancient witness to interpret the hapax directly |
| 24:67 | וְיִנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ ("and Isaac was comforted after his mother") | παρεκλήθη Ισαακ περὶ τῆς μητρὸς αὐτοῦ ("Isaac was consoled about his mother") | "So Isaac was comforted after his mother's death" | LXX uses παρακαλέω (parakaleō — the NT consolation word); formal translation adds "death" for clarity, as the Hebrew idiom implies it |

The most significant LXX variant in this chapter is the consistent rendering of חֶסֶד (hesed) as ἔλεος (eleos, "mercy") throughout, where the formal translation follows "steadfast love" as the more precise covenant term. The LXX's "mercy" is not wrong, but it narrows the covenantal-relational semantic range of hesed to its pity/compassion component, while "steadfast love" better captures the faithful-love-within-covenant dimension that the narrative foregrounds.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Longest Chapter in Genesis — Structure and Technique**

Genesis 24, with its 67 verses, is the longest chapter in Genesis and one of the most accomplished pieces of sustained narrative prose in the Hebrew Bible. Its distinctive structural feature — the retelling in verses 34–49 of events already narrated in verses 1–27 — is not a sign of editorial seams or textual conflation. It is a deliberate artistic strategy: the servant must persuade Laban and Bethuel that the entire journey is divinely ordained, and he does so by narrating the whole sequence himself, in his own voice, to establish the providential pattern. The variations in the retelling (the order of events in v. 47; the "in my heart" in v. 45; the formulation differences throughout) are realistic features of oral retelling and serve to characterize the servant as a careful, persuasive narrator.

**The Unnamed Servant — A Portrait of Faithful Agency**

The servant is never named in this chapter. Some traditions identify him as Eliezer of Damascus (Genesis 15:2), but the text of Genesis 24 nowhere makes that identification. He is consistently called "the servant" (הָעֶבֶד, ha'eved) or "the man" (הָאִישׁ, ha'ish). This anonymity is meaningful: the servant's purpose is entirely instrumental — he is the agent of Abraham's will and, more fundamentally, of YHWH's providence. His prayer at the well (vv. 12–14), his worship upon its answer (vv. 26–27), his refusal to eat before delivering his message (v. 33), his immediate request to depart after the agreement (v. 54), and his final, wordless report to Isaac (v. 66) all characterize a man whose identity is wholly subsumed in his commission. The formal translation preserves "the servant" throughout, resisting any temptation to supply a name the text deliberately withholds.

**Providence Without Intervention — The Theological Core**

Genesis 24 is striking for how it presents divine providence. There are no direct divine speeches, no theophanies, no miraculous events. The entire chapter works through the convergence of natural events: a young woman comes to the well at the right time, she has precisely the character the servant's test was designed to reveal, she is precisely the right family connection. YHWH acts through the ordinary weave of human circumstance. The servant prays; the answer arrives before he finishes praying (v. 15: "Before he had finished speaking" — וַיְהִי הוּא טֶרֶם כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר). When the servant prays a second time in his retelling (v. 45: "Before I had finished speaking in my heart"), the same formula appears. The repetition underscores the theme: YHWH's answer preceded the prayer's completion. The formal translation preserves this formula intact in both occurrences.

**Rebekah's Agency**

The chapter's final structural move — the family calling Rebekah and asking her directly whether she will go (vv. 57–58) — gives Rebekah her only line in the negotiation. Her answer, אֵלֵךְ (elekh — "I will go"), is a single word in Hebrew. It is one of the most consequential single words in the patriarchal narrative: the matriarch's free assent to the marriage that will continue the covenant line. The formal translation renders it "I will go" — the single, unelaborated affirmative that the Hebrew presents. No commentary is needed; the brevity is the point.

**The Blessing Formula (v. 60) — Echo of Genesis 22:17**

The blessing Rebekah's family pronounces over her upon departure — "May your offspring possess the gate of those who hate him!" — directly echoes the divine blessing of Abraham after the binding of Isaac in Genesis 22:17: "your offspring shall possess the gate of his enemies." The family's human blessing over Rebekah thus recapitulates the divine blessing over Abraham, binding the two covenant moments together. The formal translation renders "those who hate him" (שֹׂנְאֵיהֶם, son'eihem — literally "those who hate them/him") — the Hebrew pronominal suffix is third-person singular masculine, referring to Rebekah's offspring collectively — and preserves the specific "possess the gate" formula that echoes 22:17.
